title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a food scientist called Dr. Elena Costa talking about the history and science of spices. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a food scientist called Dr. Elena Costa talking about the history and science of spices. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Dr. Elena Costa discusses how the search for spices shaped history and how they affect our brain.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. My name is Dr. Elena Costa. I’ve always been fascinated by what we eat, but more importantly, why we eat it. [7]I first discovered my love for food science while working in my family’s restaurant as a teenager[/7]. I noticed how just a pinch of a certain spice could completely transform a dish. Spices were once the most valuable commodities on Earth. In the past, [8]the primary reason for the global spice trade was the need for food preservation[/8], long before refrigeration existed. Spices like black pepper and cloves were essential for keeping meat edible. Today, my work is more analytical. [9]I found that performing a detailed chemical analysis of cinnamon helped us understand its anti-inflammatory properties[/9]. It’s amazing how these plants have evolved to protect themselves. One of the most expensive spices in the world remains saffron. [10]We’ve started using automated harvesting drones to reduce the labor costs associated with saffron production[/10]. It’s a very delicate flower that must be picked by hand otherwise. My research also looks at the psychological impact of flavor. [11]I was surprised to find that the smell of vanilla had a measurable effect on reducing patient anxiety in hospitals[/11]. It’s a very powerful tool. However, the industry faces challenges. [12]The increasing prevalence of food fraud is the biggest concern for high-end spice importers[/12]. Sometimes cheap fillers are used to replace expensive ingredients. We are fighting this with DNA testing. Looking forward, I’m excited about urban farming. [13]I believe that vertical gardens will soon allow cities to produce their own exotic spices locally[/13]. It would significantly reduce shipping emissions. As I often say to my colleagues, [14]we are not just cooks, we are 'flavor detectives' uncovering the secrets of the natural world[/14]. It’s a journey that never ends.

dialogue.speaker: elena
dialogue.text: Hello. My name is Dr. Elena Costa. I’ve always been fascinated by what we eat, but more importantly, why we eat it. I first discovered my love for food science while working in my family’s restaurant as a teenager. I noticed how just a pinch of a certain spice could completely transform a dish. Spices were once the most valuable commodities on Earth. In the past, the primary reason for the global spice trade was the need for food preservation, long before refrigeration existed. Spices like black pepper and cloves were essential for keeping meat edible. Today, my work is more analytical. I found that performing a detailed chemical analysis of cinnamon helped us understand its anti-inflammatory properties. It’s amazing how these plants have evolved to protect themselves. One of the most expensive spices in the world remains saffron. We’ve started using automated harvesting drones to reduce the labor costs associated with saffron production. It’s a very delicate flower that must be picked by hand otherwise. My research also looks at the psychological impact of flavor. I was surprised to find that the smell of vanilla had a measurable effect on reducing patient anxiety in hospitals. It’s a very powerful tool. However, the industry faces challenges. The increasing prevalence of food fraud is the biggest concern for high-end spice importers. Sometimes cheap fillers are used to replace expensive ingredients. We are fighting this with DNA testing. Looking forward, I’m excited about urban farming. I believe that vertical gardens will soon allow cities to produce their own exotic spices locally. It would significantly reduce shipping emissions. As I often say to my colleagues, we are not just cooks, we are 'flavor detectives' uncovering the secrets of the natural world. It’s a journey that never ends.

questions.question: Elena’s interest in the science of food began during her time at a (7) .
questions.explanation: She says she discovered her love for food science while working in her family's restaurant.

questions.question: Historically, spices were mainly used for the purpose of (8) .
questions.explanation: She states that the primary reason for the spice trade was the need for food preservation.

questions.question: Elena used a process called (9) to study the health benefits of cinnamon.
questions.explanation: She mentions that performing a detailed chemical analysis helped understand its properties.

questions.question: The company is testing (10) to make the collection of saffron more efficient.
questions.explanation: They have started using automated harvesting drones to reduce labor costs.

questions.question: Elena was amazed by the ability of (11) to help people feel more relaxed.
questions.explanation: She found that the smell of vanilla reduced patient anxiety in hospitals.

questions.question: Elena identifies (12) as the most serious issue currently affecting the spice market.
questions.explanation: She states that the increasing prevalence of food fraud is the biggest concern.

questions.question: According to Elena, (13) could be the solution to the environmental cost of importing spices.
questions.explanation: She believes vertical gardens will allow cities to produce spices locally.

questions.question: Elena and her team refer to themselves as (14) due to the nature of their investigations.
questions.explanation: She says they are 'flavor detectives' uncovering the secrets of the natural world.
